title: Compute Mobius function for 1..N
description: Sieve-compute mu(n) for all n from 1 to N

examples.explanation: For N=6, compute μ(1) to μ(6): μ(1)=1. n=2: prime factors {2}, count=1, no square, μ(2)=(-1)^1=-1. n=3: {3}, count=1, μ(3)=-1. n=4: prime factors {2,2}, squared prime (2² divides 4), μ(4)=0. n=5: {5}, count=1, μ(5)=-1. n=6: prime factors {2,3}, count=2, no square, μ(6)=(-1)^2=1. Result: [μ(0) unused, 1, -1, -1, 0, -1, 1].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Prime Factorization Per Number
approaches.summary: For each number from 1 to N, compute its prime factorization, count distinct prime factors, and check for squared prime factors to determine μ(n). This approach processes each number independently without leveraging precomputed information across numbers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Mobius Function for All Numbers 1 to N Using Prime Factorization Per Number
ladders.desc: Implement a complete solution that computes μ(n) for all n from 1 to N by independently factorizing each number.
ladders.explanation: The Mobius function μ(n) is defined as: μ(1) = 1; μ(n) = 0 if n has a squared prime factor; μ(n) = (-1)^k if n is a product of k distinct primes. This approach computes μ(n) for each n from 1 to N independently by finding its prime factorization. For each number, we iterate through potential prime factors up to √n. For each prime factor p, we check if p² divides n (setting μ(n)=0 if true), otherwise count it as a distinct prime factor. After factorization, if n > 1, it means n itself is prime (one more distinct prime factor). Finally, μ(n) = (-1)^(distinct prime count) if no squared prime was found, else 0. The intuition is that μ(n) depends solely on n's prime factorization, so we can compute it directly without cross-number optimization. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize result array mu of size N+1. 2. For n from 1 to N: a. If n=1, mu[1]=1. b. Else, initialize count=0, temp=n, squared=false. c. For p from 2 to √temp: While p divides temp: If p² divides original n, set squared=true. Else if first time p divides, increment count. Divide temp by p. d. If temp>1, increment count (remaining prime). e. mu[n]=0 if squared else (-1)^count. Time: O(N * √N) worst-case (when many numbers are prime or have large prime factors). Space: O(N) for output, O(1) additional. This connects to L1 by using the prime factorization subroutine for each number. Common mistakes: Not checking p² against original n (checking against temp after division gives wrong results), forgetting to handle remaining prime factor after loop, off-by-one in loop bounds. Real code pattern: def compute_mobius(N): mu = [0]*(N+1); for n in range(1,N+1): if n==1: mu[1]=1; continue; count=0; original=n; squared=False; p=2; while p*p <= original: if original%p==0: cnt=0; while original%p==0: original//=p; cnt+=1; if cnt>=2: squared=True; break; count+=1; p+=1; if original>1: count+=1; mu[n]=0 if squared else (-1)**count; return mu.

examples.trace: For N=6, compute μ(1) to μ(6): μ(1)=1. n=2: prime factors {2}, count=1, no square, μ(2)=(-1)^1=-1. n=3: {3}, count=1, μ(3)=-1. n=4: prime factors {2,2}, squared prime (2² divides 4), μ(4)=0. n=5: {5}, count=1, μ(5)=-1. n=6: prime factors {2,3}, count=2, no square, μ(6)=(-1)^2=1. Result: [μ(0) unused, 1, -1, -1, 0, -1, 1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization of a Single Number with Square Detection
ladders.desc: Compute the prime factorization of a given integer n, counting distinct primes and detecting squared primes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine factors a number n into its prime factors, specifically tailored for computing μ(n). It needs to: 1. Count the number of distinct prime factors. 2. Detect if any prime factor has exponent ≥2 (i.e., a squared prime divides n). The intuition is that μ(n) requires knowing if n is square-free (no squared prime factor) and the parity of distinct prime count. We iterate through potential prime factors p from 2 to √n. For each p, if p divides n, we check how many times it divides: if it divides at least twice, we set a squared flag to true. Otherwise, we count it as one distinct prime. We divide n by p as many times as possible. After the loop, if n>1, it means the remaining n is a prime factor (distinct, not squared since we've removed all smaller factors). The algorithm: Input n. If n==1, return (count=0, squared=false). Initialize count=0, squared=false, temp=n. For p=2 to √temp: if temp%p==0: exponent=0; while temp%p==0: temp//=p; exponent+=1; if exponent>=2: squared=true; break; count+=1. If temp>1: count+=1. Return (count, squared). Time: O(√n) worst-case (when n is prime or product of two large primes). Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each n from 1 to N. Common mistakes: Not breaking after finding squared prime (optimization), using temp for squared check instead of original n (incorrect), missing the final prime factor. Real code pattern: def factorize(n): if n==1: return (0,False); count=0; squared=False; original=n; p=2; while p*p <= original: if original%p==0: exp=0; while original%p==0: original//=p; exp+=1; if exp>=2: squared=True; break; count+=1; p+=1; if original>1: count+=1; return (count, squared).

examples.trace: n=12. original=12. p=2: 12%2==0, divide: 12/2=6, exp=1; 6%2==0, divide: 6/2=3, exp=2. Since exp>=2, squared=True, break. count remains 0 (we break before incrementing). Return (0,True). Note: We break early because squared prime found, count irrelevant.

n=30. original=30. p=2: 30%2==0, divide: 30/2=15, exp=1; 15%2!=0. exp=1<2, count=1. p=3: 15%3==0, divide: 15/3=5, exp=1; 5%3!=0. exp=1<2, count=2. p=4 skipped (not prime, but algorithm checks divisibility, 5%4!=0). p=5: 5%5==0, divide: 5/5=1, exp=1; exp=1<2, count=3. original=1, loop ends. original=1, no remaining prime. Return (3,False).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Determining μ(n) from Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Given the count of distinct primes and whether n has a squared prime, compute μ(n).
ladders.explanation: The core logic maps the output of prime factorization to μ(n) using the definition: μ(1)=1; if n has a squared prime factor, μ(n)=0; otherwise, μ(n)=(-1)^k where k is the number of distinct prime factors. The intuition is that μ(n) encodes information about the prime structure: it's 0 for non-square-free numbers (indicating they are not products of distinct primes), and ±1 for square-free numbers, with sign depending on whether the number of primes is even or odd. This is fundamental in number theory, e.g., in Möbius inversion. Step-by-step: Input: count (distinct primes), squared (boolean). If squared is True, return 0. Else, if count is even, return 1; if odd, return -1. For n=1, handle separately: count=0, squared=False, so μ(1)=(-1)^0=1. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L1 by taking its output to produce the final μ value. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle n=1 specially (though formula works), incorrect sign for odd/even, not checking squared first. Real code pattern: def mobius_from_factors(count, squared): if squared: return 0; return 1 if count%2==0 else -1.

examples.trace: squared=False, so not zero. count=2 is even, so μ=(-1)^2=1.
squared=True, so μ=0 immediately, regardless of count.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Potential Prime Factors Up to √n
ladders.desc: For a given n, iterate through numbers from 2 to √n to find prime factors.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves looping through potential divisors p from 2 up to the square root of n to find prime factors. The intuition is that if n has a prime factor p, then p ≤ √n or n itself is prime. This reduces the search space from O(n) to O(√n). We don't need to check only primes; checking all integers works because if a composite number divides n, its prime factors would have been found earlier. However, for efficiency in factorization, we typically increment p by 1, but optimized methods might skip evens. Step-by-step: Input n. Initialize p=2. While p*p <= n: Check if n%p==0 (p is a factor). Then, to fully divide out p, use a while loop: while n%p==0: n//=p. After processing p, increment p by 1. After the loop, if n>1, then n is a prime factor (the last one). This is used in L1 to find factors. Time: O(√n) iterations. Space: O(1). Common mistakes: Using p <= n instead of p*p <= n (inefficient), not updating n during division (infinite loop), forgetting the final prime factor. Real code pattern: def iterate_factors(n): p=2; while p*p <= n: if n%p==0: while n%p==0: n//=p; p+=1; if n>1: # n is a prime factor.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Divisibility and Dividing Out Factors
ladders.desc: Given integers a and b, check if b divides a, and if so, divide a by b repeatedly.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key actions: 1. Checking divisibility: Determine if b divides a (i.e., a % b == 0). 2. Dividing out: While b divides a, replace a with a/b. This is fundamental in factorization to extract all occurrences of a prime factor. The intuition is that to factor a number, we need to remove all copies of a prime factor to move on to the next potential factor. For example, to factor 12, we check if 2 divides 12 (yes), then divide 12 by 2 to get 6, check again (2 divides 6), divide to get 3, and now 2 does not divide 3, so we stop. This ensures we count the exponent of the prime. Step-by-step: Input a, b. While a % b == 0: a = a // b. Return the updated a. Time: O(log_b(a)) in the worst case (when b is a small prime and a is a power of b). Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 as part of the factor iteration loop. Common mistakes: Using floating-point division instead of integer division, not updating a in the loop (infinite loop), not checking divisibility before dividing. Real code pattern: def divide_out(a, b): while a % b == 0: a //= b; return a.

examples.trace: Start a=24. Check 24%2==0, true: a=24//2=12. Check 12%2==0, true: a=12//2=6. Check 6%2==0, true: a=6//2=3. Check 3%2==0, false. Stop. Final a=3.
Start a=15. Check 15%3==0, true: a=15//3=5. Check 5%3==0, false. Stop. Final a=5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Learn what prime numbers are and how to check if one number divides another.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two key concepts: 1. Prime numbers: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Non-primes (composites) like 4, 6, 8 have other divisors. 1 is neither prime nor composite. 2. Divisibility: A number a is divisible by b (b divides a) if there exists an integer k such that a = b * k. We check this using the modulo operator: a % b == 0. For example, 10 is divisible by 2 because 10 % 2 == 0, but 10 is not divisible by 3 because 10 % 3 == 1. The intuition is that factorization breaks a number into primes, and divisibility tests help find those primes. This is prerequisite for all higher levels. Step-by-step for checking if n is prime: If n <= 1, return false. For i from 2 to √n: if n % i == 0, return false. Return true. For checking divisibility: Simply compute a % b. Time for primality: O(√n). Space: O(1). This connects to L4 as the basic operation uses divisibility. Common mistakes: Thinking 1 is prime, not checking up to √n for primality (inefficient), confusion between / and % operators. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<=1: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True. def divides(a,b): return a%b==0.

examples.trace: n=7 >1. i=2: 7%2=1 !=0. i=3: 7%3=1 !=0. i=4: 4*4=16>7, stop. No divisors found, so prime.
a=12, b=4. Compute 12 % 4 = 0, so yes, divisible.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Linear Sieve with Mobius Computation
approaches.summary: Use a linear sieve to compute the smallest prime factor (spf) for all numbers up to N, then compute μ(n) using dynamic programming based on spf. This leverages the multiplicative property of μ(n) and processes numbers in linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Mobius Function for 1..N Using Linear Sieve
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear sieve with SPF to compute μ(n) for all n from 1 to N.
ladders.explanation: This solution computes the Möbius function μ(n) for all integers from 1 to N using a linear sieve approach. The Möbius function is defined as: μ(1) = 1; μ(n) = 0 if n has a squared prime factor; μ(n) = (-1)^k if n is a product of k distinct primes. The linear sieve efficiently finds the smallest prime factor (SPF) for each number up to N in O(N) time. Then, using dynamic programming, we compute μ(n) based on SPF and the multiplicative property of μ. For each number i from 2 to N, let p = spf[i]. If i is divisible by p^2, μ(i) = 0 (since it has a squared prime factor). Otherwise, μ(i) = -μ(i/p) (since multiplying by a new prime flips the sign). This leverages that μ is multiplicative: μ(ab) = μ(a)μ(b) when gcd(a,b)=1. The algorithm processes each number exactly once, giving O(N) time and O(N) space. Connection: This integrates SPF computation (L1) with the DP logic (L2) to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize μ[1]=1; incorrectly handling the p^2 condition; using O(N log N) sieve instead of linear. Real code pattern: Initialize arrays spf and mu of size N+1; set mu[1]=1; for i from 2 to N: if spf[i]==0 (prime), set spf[i]=i, mu[i]=-1; for each prime p ≤ spf[i] and i*p ≤ N: set spf[i*p]=p; if p == spf[i]: mu[i*p]=0; else: mu[i*p] = -mu[i]; return mu array.

examples.trace: Initialize: spf=[0,0,0,0,0,0,0], mu=[0,1,0,0,0,0,0]. i=2: spf[2]=0 (prime), set spf[2]=2, mu[2]=-1. For p=2 (only prime so far), i*p=4 ≤6: set spf[4]=2; since p==spf[2], mu[4]=0. i=3: spf[3]=0, set spf[3]=3, mu[3]=-1. For p=2: i*p=6 ≤6: set spf[6]=2; p=2 != spf[3]=3, so mu[6]=-mu[3]=1. For p=3: i*p=9 >6, stop. i=4: spf[4]=2 (not prime). For p=2: i*p=8 >6, stop. i=5: spf[5]=0, set spf[5]=5, mu[5]=-1. For p=2: i*p=10 >6, stop. i=6: spf[6]=2. For p=2: i*p=12 >6, stop. Result mu: [1, -1, -1, 0, -1, 1].

Initialize: spf size 11, mu[1]=1. i=2: prime, spf[2]=2, mu[2]=-1; set spf[4]=2, mu[4]=0. i=3: prime, spf[3]=3, mu[3]=-1; set spf[6]=2 (since p=2 first), mu[6]=-mu[3]=1; set spf[9]=3, mu[9]=0 (p==spf[3]). i=4: spf[4]=2; set spf[8]=2, mu[8]=0. i=5: prime, spf[5]=5, mu[5]=-1; set spf[10]=2, mu[10]=-mu[5]=1. i=6: spf[6]=2; set spf[12]>10 stop. i=7: prime, spf[7]=7, mu[7]=-1. i=8: spf[8]=2; set spf[16]>10 stop. i=9: spf[9]=3; set spf[18]>10 stop. i=10: spf[10]=2; set spf[20]>10 stop. Result mu: indices 1..10: [1, -1, -1, 0, -1, 1, -1, 0, 0, 1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Smallest Prime Factor (SPF) Using Linear Sieve
ladders.desc: Implement linear sieve to find SPF for all numbers from 1 to N.
ladders.explanation: The linear sieve computes the smallest prime factor (SPF) for each integer from 1 to N in O(N) time. Unlike the classic Sieve of Eratosthenes which marks multiples and may process numbers multiple times, the linear sieve ensures each composite number is marked exactly once by its smallest prime factor. Intuition: Maintain a list of primes found so far. For each i from 2 to N, if spf[i]==0, i is prime, so set spf[i]=i and add i to primes list. Then, for each prime p in primes such that p ≤ spf[i] and i*p ≤ N: set spf[i*p]=p. The condition p ≤ spf[i] ensures that i*p is marked by its smallest prime factor (since if p > spf[i], then spf[i] would be smaller and would have marked i*p earlier). This guarantees each composite is marked once. Time: O(N) as each number is processed once when setting spf. Space: O(N) for spf array plus O(N/log N) for primes list. Connection: SPF array is essential for L0 to compute μ(n) efficiently. Common mistakes: Using i*p ≤ N as the only condition (missing p ≤ spf[i]), leading to O(N log log N) time; not initializing spf[1]=0 or 1 appropriately. Real code pattern: spf = [0]*(N+1); primes = []; for i in range(2, N+1): if spf[i]==0: spf[i]=i; primes.append(i); for p in primes: if p > spf[i] or i*p > N: break; spf[i*p]=p.

examples.trace: Initialize spf[0..10]=0. i=2: spf[2]=0 → prime, set spf[2]=2, primes=[2]. For p=2: i*p=4 ≤10, set spf[4]=2; p=2 ≤ spf[2]=2, continue; next p none. i=3: spf[3]=0 → prime, set spf[3]=3, primes=[2,3]. For p=2: i*p=6 ≤10, set spf[6]=2; p=2 ≤ spf[3]=3, continue; p=3: i*p=9 ≤10, set spf[9]=3; p=3 ≤ spf[3]=3, continue; next p none. i=4: spf[4]=2 (not prime). For p=2: i*p=8 ≤10, set spf[8]=2; p=2 ≤ spf[4]=2, continue; p=3: i*p=12 >10 break. i=5: prime, set spf[5]=5, primes=[2,3,5]; mark 10 with spf[10]=2. i=6: spf[6]=2; mark 12>10 break. i=7: prime, set spf[7]=7; mark 14>10 break. i=8: spf[8]=2; mark 16>10 break. i=9: spf[9]=3; mark 18>10 break. i=10: spf[10]=2; mark 20>10 break. Result spf indices 0..10: [0,0,2,3,2,5,2,7,2,3,2].

i=2: prime, spf[2]=2; mark 4,6,8,10,12,14. i=3: prime, spf[3]=3; mark 6,9,12,15. i=4: spf[4]=2; mark 8,12. i=5: prime, spf[5]=5; mark 10,15. i=6: spf[6]=2; mark 12. i=7: prime, spf[7]=7; mark 14. i=8: spf[8]=2; mark 16>15 break. i=9: spf[9]=3; mark 18>15 break. i=10: spf[10]=2; mark 20>15 break. i=11: prime, spf[11]=11; mark 22>15 break. i=12: spf[12]=2; mark 24>15 break. i=13: prime, spf[13]=13; mark 26>15 break. i=14: spf[14]=2; mark 28>15 break. i=15: spf[15]=3; mark 30>15 break. Final spf: indices 0..15 as above.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute μ(n) from SPF Using Dynamic Programming
ladders.desc: Use SPF and DP to compute μ(n) based on multiplicative property.
ladders.explanation: Given the smallest prime factor (SPF) array for numbers 1..N, compute the Möbius function μ(n) using dynamic programming. Intuition: μ is multiplicative: μ(ab) = μ(a)μ(b) when gcd(a,b)=1. For a number n with smallest prime factor p, we can express n = p * m, where m = n/p. Then: If p^2 divides n (i.e., spf[m] == p), then n has a squared prime factor, so μ(n)=0. Otherwise, n is p times a number m that is not divisible by p, so μ(n) = -μ(m) because adding a new prime factor flips the sign (since μ(p) = -1 for prime p). Base case: μ(1)=1. Algorithm: Initialize mu[1]=1. For i from 2 to N: p = spf[i]; m = i/p; if spf[m] == p: mu[i] = 0; else: mu[i] = -mu[m]. This works because when processing i in increasing order, m < i, so mu[m] is already computed. Time: O(N) as each i is processed once. Space: O(N) for mu array. Connection: This logic uses SPF from L1 to compute μ for L0. Common mistakes: Not handling the case where m=1 (i is prime) correctly—for prime i, m=1, spf[1] is not p, so mu[i] = -mu[1] = -1, which is correct; incorrectly checking p^2 divides n as (i % (p*p) == 0) which is slower; forgetting to set mu[1]=1. Real code pattern: mu = [0]*(N+1); mu[1]=1; for i in range(2, N+1): p = spf[i]; m = i // p; if spf[m] == p: mu[i] = 0; else: mu[i] = -mu[m].

ladders.title: Building Block — Multiplicative Property of Möbius Function
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the multiplicative property μ(ab) = μ(a)μ(b) for coprime a,b.
ladders.explanation: The Möbius function μ(n) is multiplicative: for any two positive integers a and b with gcd(a,b)=1, μ(ab) = μ(a)μ(b). Intuition: This property arises from the prime factorization. If a and b share no prime factors, then the prime factors of ab are the union of those of a and b. Since μ(n) depends only on the parity of the number of distinct prime factors and the presence of squared factors, and since squared factors in ab occur only if they occur in a or b individually, the property holds. Formally: μ(1)=1; if n has a squared prime factor, μ(n)=0; otherwise, μ(n)=(-1)^k where k is the number of distinct prime factors. For coprime a and b, the prime factors are disjoint, so the number of distinct primes in ab is sum of those in a and b, and squared factors in ab exist iff they exist in a or b. Thus, μ(ab) = (-1)^{k_a+k_b} = (-1)^{k_a}(-1)^{k_b} = μ(a)μ(b). This property allows efficient computation: to compute μ(n), we can break n into coprime parts. In our approach, we use the smallest prime factor p: let n = p * m. Since p is prime and if p does not divide m (i.e., gcd(p,m)=1), then μ(n) = μ(p)μ(m) = (-1) * μ(m). If p divides m, then n has p^2 as factor, so μ(n)=0. This reduces the problem to computing μ for smaller m. Time/space: Understanding this property is conceptual, but applying it gives O(1) per number in DP. Connection: This property is the foundation for the DP logic in L2. Common mistakes: Assuming multiplicativity holds for non-coprime numbers (it doesn't—e.g., μ(4)=0, μ(2)=-1, but μ(8)=0 ≠ μ(4)μ(2)); misapplying the sign flip. Real code pattern: This is a mathematical insight, but in code: if n % (p*p) == 0: mu=0; else: mu = -mu[n//p] given p is smallest prime.

examples.trace: Compute μ(2): 2 is prime, so μ(2) = -1. Compute μ(3): 3 is prime, so μ(3) = -1. Since gcd(2,3)=1, multiplicative property applies: μ(6) = μ(2)μ(3) = (-1)*(-1) = 1. Verify directly: 6 = 2*3, two distinct primes, no squared factor, so μ(6)=(-1)^2=1. Match.

Compute μ(4): 4=2^2, has squared prime factor, so μ(4)=0. Compute μ(5): prime, so μ(5)=-1. Since gcd(4,5)=1, multiplicative property applies: μ(20) = μ(4)μ(5) = 0*(-1) = 0. Verify directly: 20=2^2*5, has squared factor 2^2, so μ(20)=0. Match. Note: Even though 5 is prime, the presence of squared factor in 4 makes the product zero.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Smallest Prime Factor of a Number
ladders.desc: Given a number n, find its smallest prime factor (SPF).
ladders.explanation: The smallest prime factor (SPF) of a positive integer n > 1 is the smallest prime number that divides n. For example, SPF(6)=2, SPF(7)=7, SPF(9)=3. Intuition: Every composite number n has a prime factor ≤ √n, so we can test divisibility by primes starting from 2 up to √n. If no prime divides n, then n is prime and its SPF is n itself. This operation is fundamental for prime factorization and sieve algorithms. Algorithm: If n < 2, return None or 1. For i from 2 to √n: if n % i == 0, return i (since i is the smallest divisor and must be prime because if i were composite, its smaller prime factor would have divided n earlier). If no divisor found, return n (n is prime). Time: O(√n) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection: This operation is performed repeatedly in naive approaches, but in linear sieve (L1), we compute SPF for all numbers efficiently. Common mistakes: Not handling n=1 correctly; checking up to n instead of √n (inefficient); not realizing that the first divisor found is prime. Real code pattern: def spf(n): if n < 2: return 1; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: return i; return n.

examples.trace: n=12. Check i=2: 12 % 2 == 0 → true, so smallest prime factor is 2. Return 2. No need to check further.

n=17. Check i=2: 17 % 2 = 1 ≠0. i=3: 17 % 3 = 2 ≠0. i=4: not prime, skip or check but 17 % 4 =1 ≠0. Continue up to √17≈4.12, so i=4 last. Since no divisor found, 17 is prime, so SPF is 17 itself. Return 17.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, composite numbers, and basic divisibility.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. A composite number is a positive integer greater than 1 that is not prime, meaning it has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself. Examples: 4, 6, 8, 9, 10. Divisibility: An integer a divides integer b (written a | b) if there exists an integer k such that b = a*k. For example, 2 divides 6 because 6=2*3. Key properties: Every integer greater than 1 is either prime or composite; 1 is neither prime nor composite. Prime factorization: Every composite number can be expressed as a product of prime numbers uniquely (up to order). Intuition: Primes are the building blocks of numbers. In the context of Möbius function, we care about prime factors to determine if a number has squared factors or count distinct primes. Time/space: Conceptual, but checking divisibility is O(1) per test. Connection: This foundational knowledge is needed to understand SPF (L4) and the multiplicative property (L3). Common mistakes: Thinking 1 is prime; not recognizing that 2 is the only even prime; confusing divisibility with factors. Real code pattern: To check if n is prime: if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: 7 > 1. Check divisibility by integers from 2 to √7≈2.65. i=2: 7 % 2 = 1 ≠0. i=3: 7 % 3 = 1 ≠0. No divisors found, so 7 is prime.
9 > 1. Check divisibility: i=2: 9 % 2 = 1 ≠0. i=3: 9 % 3 = 0 → divisible, so 9 has a divisor 3 other than 1 and itself. Therefore, 9 is composite.
